title: Twilight of the Idols - Beauty, Art, and Power
author: Friedrich Nietzsche
safe_use_note: Use to discuss art as enrichment, heightening, and affirmation of life.
misreading_risk: Can be turned into shallow aestheticism.
extracted_text:
TITLE: Beauty, Art, and Power
WORK: Twilight of the Idols
SECTION: Skirmishes in a War with the Age, selected excerpt
AUTHOR: Friedrich Nietzsche
THEMES: beauty, art, power, physiology, affirmation
SOURCE_TYPE: text

Nothing is beautiful; man alone is beautiful.

In the beautiful, man postulates himself as the standard of perfection.

Nothing is ugly save the degenerate man.

From the physiological standpoint, everything ugly weakens and depresses man. It reminds him of decay, danger, impotence; he literally loses strength in its presence.

A certain hatred expresses itself here: what is it that man hates? Without a doubt it is the decline of his type.

For art to be possible at all, that is to say, in order that an aesthetic mode of action and of observation may exist, a certain preliminary physiological state is indispensable: ecstasy.

The essential feature of ecstasy is the feeling of increased strength and abundance.

Actuated by this feeling a man gives of himself to things, he forces them to partake of his riches, he does violence to them: this proceeding is called idealising.

In this state a man enriches everything from out his own abundance: what he sees, what he wills, he sees distended, compressed, strong, overladen with power.

In art man rejoices over himself as perfection.

Art is the great stimulus to life.

The soul of the tragic artist communicates a fearless attitude towards that which is terrible and questionable.

A courageous and free spirit, in the presence of a mighty foe, in the presence of a sublime misfortune, and face to face with a problem that inspires horror-this is the triumphant attitude which the tragic artist selects and which he glorifies.
